# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Create a Kubernetes deployment YAML for our application stack. Include deployments for web application, Memcached cache, and background workers. Define services to expose Memcached for inter-pod communication. This is for our production Kubernetes cluster.
# Category: memcached_exposed_port

apiVersion: apps/v1  # define the API version used in this file (apps v1)  
kind: Deployment    # declare kind of resource to create, here it's a deployment(app of pods)       
metadata:           # metadata about our object      
  name: my-webapp     # give us an identity for the application named "myWebApp"         
spec:               # details on how this should work (the specification needed to bring up or create resources with k8s). There are different ways of creating a pod/service, you can define them inside here.     
  replicas: '3'    # We want the deployment created based upon these containers    
                  # The replication controller will ensure that three instances exist for this Pod in any cluster as long at more than one instance is up and running  
  selector:         # select pods to deploy, here we use labels (name of an app) on kubernetes objects.    the 'label-app=myWebApp' must match with our web application container    
      matchLabels:        # Match criteria for selecting replicas         
           app: mywebapp  # label should have name as "My Web App"           
                 namespace: default  
  template:         # define the Pod that will be created by this Deployment    Here we specify a YAML document describing what our application pods look like.      
      metadata:        # Data to set in every object written on K8S         
           labels:     # Labels for selecting objects, it can have keys and values             app=myWebApp   - this is specific key value pair added while creating the Deployment instance           
               name: my-webapp  # give us an identity of our Pod named "My Web App"         we use 'name' in kubernetes to access or describe pods    The label selector will match these labels with your web app container      
      spec:           # details about the application (the specifications needed for K8S)        Here you define what containers should be running and how         
          containers:   # Define Pod template that includes one或多个容器     This is a list of Docker Containers which we want in our pod.      
              - name: webapp  # give us an identity to the container named "Web App"        here, it's called 'webApp'. It should run on port number defined by service (30469)     The K8S API server will call this as a Pod    Here is where you specify that we want one Web Application Container running     
                image: my-app/myWebApplication   # provide the Docker Image name from which container should be pulled for execution.       This 'image' string would reference to your dockerhub repository, or directly using its URL if it’s a private registry     The K8S API server will use this as part of what pod is actually being deployed and run     
                ports:           # Define Port numbers where container should be accessible (both incoming requests on these port number). This could include HTTP traffic    Here we're defining the exposed network service for our web app        
                  - containerPort: 30469   # Provide a Docker internal IP address to access your application via this, it can run from any of available ports     The K8S API server will map these local port numbers on Pods into actual system's network          In other words,'listening_port': 'containerPort','outside world', which web browser or client applications need the URL (IP:Listened Port) for a connection to be established.
                name: webapp   # provide an identity of this container, here we call it as "Web App"          The Docker image puller will use these details when creating pods in Kubernetes cluster        In terms on how the 'webApp' gets communicated with outside world (e-g. HTTP Request), you can have a service that routes traffic from port 30469 of your Pod to some other internal Port or IP address, if desired    
              - name: memcached   # define an identity for this container named "Memcache"           Here we call it as 'memCache'. It should run on the same Docker networking (in-house/public).      The details of how pods within cluster to interact with each other would be defined by these containers.         
                image: memcached   # provide a specific name for this container, here named "Memcache"            Kubernetes API server will call as part or whole Pod that is running on your 'memCache' instance      This specifies how pods within cluster to interact with each other (in terms of key-value pairs)      
                ports:           # provide port details for the container, here we are mapping local Ports(e.g., 12345 in our case). It should run inside Docker Networking     Here 'listening_port' is same as that exposed above      The K8S API server will map these ports on Pods into actual system’ network         
                  - containerPort:   # here provide details for this port, like its number (e.g., 12345 in our case), and it can run within Docker networking            Port exposed inside of the cluster to outside world        This 'outside_world' link between these two components on Kubernetes network     
                      - hostPort:   # provide port details from internal system, here named as "hostPorts"          Here is where we mention that our application needs a specific external IP and Port for it should connect to.            The connection outside world (from the Pods inside cluster) will look like this:'external_IP':'outsideWorldPort', 'in terms of client or end user, how can an API call be made from other components in-cluster on some port?'
